title: Cheese and spinach borek
subtitle: Turkish filo pastry rolls with feta and wilted spinach
excerpt: Twelve cigar-shaped filo rolls filled with feta and wilted spinach, brushed with butter and baked until shatteringly crisp. The filling is made in minutes; the filo goes straight from the packet. Serve as a starter or alongside soup.
sourceNotes: Borek is a family of filled pastry dishes documented across Ottoman, Turkish, Balkan, and Middle Eastern cookery from at least the medieval period. Recipes appear in pre-1928 Turkish and Balkan household cookery texts and in early-twentieth-century accounts of Ottoman domestic cooking available through Turkish national library archives. The filo-wrapped cigar form with cheese and greens filling is attested in multiple pre-1928 regional cookery sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze shaped and unbaked. Bake from frozen at the same temperature, adding 5 to 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Use two baking trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 4 hours ahead, keep covered in the fridge, and bake just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 200°C. The rolls should be evenly golden all over; check the undersides at 20 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Filo pastry dries out within minutes of being exposed to air, becoming brittle and difficult to roll without cracking. Keep the sheets you are not working with covered with a slightly damp tea towel. Brush each layer lightly with melted butter or oil before adding the next; the fat keeps the sheets pliable during baking and creates the crisp, flaky layers. A light hand is better than a heavy one: too much butter and the pastry becomes greasy rather than crisp.

content.text: Borek are Turkish pastries built from layers of

brushed with butter. This version rolls the sheets into cigars around a feta and spinach filling. Twelve rolls. Keep the filo covered while you work; it dries and cracks if left exposed for more than a minute.

Wilt the spinach in a dry frying pan over high heat for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring frequently. Tip into a colander and press out as much liquid as possible with the back of a spoon; squeeze again by hand once cool enough to handle. Chop roughly. Mix with the crumbled feta, eggs, pepper, and nutmeg if using. The filling should be quite dry; excess moisture will make the filo soggy.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan (200°C conventional). Line a baking tray with paper. Lay one filo sheet on a clean surface and brush lightly with melted butter. Place a second sheet on top and brush again. Lay a line of filling (about 2 heaped tablespoons) along the short edge, leaving 2 cm clear at each side. Fold the sides in over the filling, then roll up tightly into a cigar. Place seam-side down on the tray. Repeat with the remaining sheets and filling. Brush all the rolls with the remaining butter.
Bake for 22 to 25 minutes, until the rolls are evenly golden and crisp. Serve immediately; they soften as they sit.
